data.title: Qiagen gains on report it's weighing options amid takeover interest
data.description:
Qiagen (QGEN) rose 12% after a report that the European molecular testing firm is evaluating strategic options, including a sale, amid new takeover interest. 

The company is working with advisers as its supervisory board gets preliminary interest from potential buyers, according to a Bloomberg report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Qiagen (QGEN) has held discussions with several possible bidders in recent weeks, including some US strategic buyers. 

Deliberations are said to be at an early stage, and there's no certainty that talks will lead to a deal. The latest round of talks comes as Qiagen has attracted takeover interest on multiple occasions in recent years.

Qiagen declined to comment to Bloomberg.

data.title: Citigroup CEO says credit card interest caps would restrict access to credit, hurt economy
data.description:
Citigroup (C) Chair and CEO Jane Fraser doesn't expect Congress to pass legislation that would cap interest on credit card debt at 10%, as the damage to the economy would be significant, she said in a televised interview on Tuesday.

She doesn't see bipartisan support for such a move. "I think there's a very keen understanding that this would have the opposite impact of what the actual intent would be, and there are better ways to go about it," she said in a CNBC interview on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum in Davos.

Earlier this month, President Donald Trump said he'd impose a 10% cap on credit card interest rates for one year to keep consumers from being “ripped off” by credit card companies. Fraser thinks the president is right to focus on affordability because some consumers are experiencing a “tough” situation. But if a cap were placed on credit card interest, "you would not be able to provide credit and access to credit for people who need it the most," she said.

Still, she suggested the rate cap wouldn't help. "This would not be good for the economy if this were to happen," she said. Imposing a limit on credit card interest rates would reduce spending with a particular focus on airlines, hotels, retailers, and restaurants, which have a large amount of revenue come in through credit cards.

Fraser pointed to the Carter administration when this was last tried. "Within two months, it was completely reversed," she said. "That's because caps do the opposite of what you think they would and what they intend to do. They really restrict access to credit."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: RFK Jr. has reportedly removed four members of vaccine injury panel
data.description:
Health and Human Services ((HHS)) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has dismissed at least four of the Advisory Commission on Childhood Vaccines ((ACCV)) before their terms expired, The Hill reported.

The eight-member panel issues recommendations regarding which vaccines and conditions should be included in the federal Vaccine Injury Compensation Program, a division established within HHS to shield vaccine makers from most lawsuits claiming vaccine-related injury.

Clinician and pediatric researcher Wendy Lane, pediatrician Natasha Burgert, and pediatrician Joshua Williams have all had their terms prematurely ended ahead of the scheduled end to their tenures.

Stat News previously reported that Lane and Veronica McNally, an attorney who is also director of trial advocacy at Michigan State University, received emails on Tuesday informing them of their removals.

The dismissals draw parallels with RFK Jr.’s unilateral decision to fire all 17 members of the CDC’s Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices ((ACIP)) in June 2025, citing conflicts of interest and other concerns.

Under Kennedy, a well-known vaccine skeptic, the CDC has made several notable changes to the U.S. immunization schedule, including a decision to recommend fewer vaccines for most American children this year.

Leading vaccine makers: GSK (GSK), Sanofi (SNY), Pfizer (PFE), Moderna (MRNA), BioNTech (BNTX), Novavax (NVAX), Merck (MRK), AstraZeneca (AZN)

data.title: Turkey in talks with Chevron for joint oil and gas exploration - Bloomberg
data.description:
Turkey's state energy company is in talks with Chevron (CVX) to jointly explore for oil and gas, Bloomberg reported Tuesday, citing a Turkish official familiar with the discussions.

Turkish Petroleum would work with Chevron (CVX) on seismic studies and drilling, the report said, although it is not clear which projects the companies might see some collaboration; TPAO is active in the Black Sea, Iraq, Russia and Somalia, and previously drilled in the eastern Mediterranean Sea, while Chevron operates fields offshore Israel and Cyprus.

The potential agreement is the latest move by Turkey to boost energy production and comes as ties improve with the U.S., and would follow a recent agreement with Exxon Mobil on joint exploration in the Black Sea and Mediterranean.

Separately, UBS analysts reiterated a Buy rating and $197 price target on Chevron (CVX) as the company prepares to expand production capacity at the Leviathan natural gas field offshore Israel.

data.title: Moon Capital Management adds Molina Healthcare in Q4 2025
data.description:
• Moon Capital Management initiated a position in Molina Healthcare (MOH) in Q4 2025.
  • "The stock traded as high as $300 per share only six months ago; we purchased our shares at approximately $160 in late December," said the fund letter.
  • Source: Fund Letter

data.title: AI startup Humans& raises $480M at $4.48B valuation: report
data.description:
Artificial intelligence startup Humans& has raised $480M in seed funding that values the company at a $4.48B valuation, The New York Times reported.

The three-month company did not confirm the amount of money it raised on its sparsely populated website. However, it acknowledged that Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN) co-founder Jeff Bezos, Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) Ventures and Laurene Powell Jobs' Emerson Collective are among its investors. 

Described as a “human-centric frontier AI lab,” Humans& said it believes AI “can be reimagined, centering around people and their relationships with each other.” The company hopes to do this with software.

“This requires rethinking everything about how we train models at scale and how people interact with AI,” the startup explained. “This needs innovations in long-horizon and multi-agent reinforcement learning, memory, and user understanding. At humans&, we will tightly integrate science and product development to drive this new paradigm.”

“At its best, AI should serve as a deeper connective tissue that strengthens organizations and communities,” the company added.

Some of its founders have formerly worked at xAI (X.AI), Anthropic (ANTHRO), Google DeepMind, OpenAI (OPENAI), Meta (META), Reflection, AI2, Stanford, and MIT. 

Humans& did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Lululemon founder wants to remove Advent directors from board - report
data.description:
Lululemon founder Chip Wilson wants to remove board directors from private equity firm Advent as part of a proxy fight he's waging at the athletic apparel company.

Wilson won't consider any settlement with Lululemon unless Advent's two legacy directors, including David Mussafer, step down, according to a Semafor report  on Monday, which cited people familiar with Wilson's thinking.  

A spokesperson for Wilson declined to comment to Semafor and Lululemon also declined to comment. 

Wilson joins prominent activist Elliott Investment Management in pushing for leadership changes at the struggling company as it begins the search for a new chief executive to replace Calvin McDonald. Elliott’s top choice will likely be Jane Nielsen who is currently acting as advisor to the firm.

Shares of Lululemon fell 1.5%.

data.title: Bitcoin, other crypto prices drop amid Trump's Greenland threats
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and other cryptos retreated alongside U.S. equities and Treasury bonds, amid renewed geopolitical uncertainty after U.S. President Donald Trump doubled down on his desire for U.S. control of Greenland, adding to an already fragile market mood.

The highest-profile token (BTC-USD) slid to as low as $89.8K earlier in the session, before paring losses to $90.6K, down 2.1% as of 11:15 a.m. ET. Ether (ETH-USD), the second-largest coin by market cap, gapped down 5% to $3.02K.

At the same time, Wall Street’s major market averages dropped and Treasury yields jumped, as the standoff between the U.S. and Greenland continued to heat up. Investors did, however, bought gold (XAUUSD:CUR), a and pushed the traditional safe haven's price to a new record.

"Bitcoin (BTC-USD) currently reacts to headlines, and in this environment, both large holders and retail investors tend to leave risk assets," said Gracy Chen, CEO at crypto exchange Bitget. "That happens even with the “digital gold” narrative in place. When policy risk rises, liquidity usually moves first, the rest follow later."

"While spot ETF flows were positive last week, this week may look different. With tariffs back in focus, capital is likely to rotate toward defensive assets, where Gold dominates," she added.

Meanwhile, crypto-linked stocks were decidedly lower at press time. Strategy (MSTR), which recent beefed up its bitcoin-buying strategy, tumbled 7%, Coinbase Global (COIN) -3.6%, Gemini Space Station (GEMI) -4.5%, Bakkt Holdings (BKKT) -20%, MARA Holdings (MARA) -5.6%, Riot Platforms (RIOT) -3%, American Bitcoin (ABTC) -3.1%, Bit Digital (BTBT) -3.2% and Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR) -7.3%.

data.title: Bakkt stock dives after estimated cash drops, announces new ATM program
data.description:
Bakkt Holdings (BKKT) stock sank 18% in Tuesday morning trading after the digital asset infrastructure company disclosed declining cash holdings and announced a new at-the-market equity program. The reduced cash holdings come as the pivots from its original focus of running a loyalty program based on bitcoin to a pure-play crypto infrastructure company.

The company, which provides technology for companies to use digital assets, filed to establish an ATM equity program that allows it to raise up to $300M in gross proceeds from the sale of common stock. The ATM is intended to increase its financial flexibility, allowing it to accelerate its long-term growth strategy. That includes expanding distribution of its Bakkt Agent initiative, scaling Zaira’s stablecoin payments platform, increasing investment in the Bakkt Global initiative by accelerating its previously announced expansion plans in Japan and India, and entering additional high-potential markets, the company said.

It also disclosed on Tuesday that its available cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash at Dec. 31, 2025, are estimated at $26M-$28M, down from $64.4M at Sept. 30, 2025. Its net cash used in operating activities in Q4 included a customer deposit return of ~$6.3M and legal and other professional fees related to eliminating its predecessor umbrella partnership-C corporation structure.

Use of net cash in investing activities included ~$23.8M paid in connection with the sale of its loyalty business and $4.8M of advances to the loyalty business buyer. Those were partly offset by a $10.6M cash settlement from a derivative contract and ~4.9M of proceeds from the sale of available for sale securities.

In addition, Bakkt (BKKT) estimated its Q4 gross digital asset revenue at $298M-$300M and its total digital asset costs and execution, clearing and brokerage fees at $297M-$299M, indicating that its cost of sales in Q4 2025 was nearly as much as its revenue.

data.title: Natural gas futures skyrocket as cold snap sweeps across U.S.
data.description:
U.S. natural gas futures soared Tuesday after a blast of freezing weather swept across large parts of the country, with weekend weather forecasts showing much colder-than-normal temperatures, likely leading to a spike in heating demand and electricity generation needs.

A big winter storm is expected to dump heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain across the southern Rockies, Plains and the southern U.S. by Friday, and then is forecast to shift toward the east coast through the weekend.

The cold spell upends what had been a tepid winter, which along with rising Appalachia and Permian gas production, had driven a nearly one-third decline in natural gas prices from their December peak.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for February delivery +24.1% to a YTD high $3.853/MMBtu.

Among natural gas producers: Comstock Resources (CRK) +5.2%, Expand Energy (EXE) +4.7%, Range Resources (RRC) +3.5%, Coterra Energy (CTRA) +3.3%, Antero Resources (AR) +3.1%, EQT (EQT) +1.7%.

"The frigid weather pivot comes with speculator short positions at a 14-month high, suggesting further bullish risks as shorts are forced to buy back gas," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics said in a note, adding sub-zero temperatures threaten production freeze-offs in the Marcellus and Bakken shale areas.

"Volatility will stay high, however, and any weather model warming into mid-February could allow a near term price spike to eventually soften," EBW wrote.

ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLU)
